claim.hypotheses_location: Page 1, Abstract – “Accordingly, it was hypothesized that people would increase their intimacy with a partner who was attentive to them.”

data.source: Scenario questionnaires (Studies 1a & 1b) and computer-mediated laboratory experiments (Studies 2a–2c)
data.sample_size: Total N ≈ 526 participants (Study 1a: 312; Study 1b: 105; Study 2a: 29; Study 2b: 44; Study 2c: 36)
data.notes: Study 1 used written scenarios; Study 2 used a simulated partner via computer program to manipulate attention independently of material benefit.

method.description: Across two scenario experiments and three laboratory experiments, the authors manipulated a partner’s attentiveness toward participants while holding material benefits constant. Participants then reported perceived intimacy and related variables.
steps: Recruit undergraduate participants for each study.
Present written support scenarios (Study 1) or a joint trivia task with an ostensible partner (Study 2).
Manipulate key independent variables: partner attention (present/absent or frequency levels), benefit (present/absent), and time (long/short) where applicable.
Collect self-report measures: intimacy items, expectation of partner help, willingness to help, or interest in friendship.
Analyze data using ANOVA, t-tests, and mediation (Sobel) analyses to test effects of attention on intimacy and downstream outcomes.
method.models: Factorial ANOVA, independent-samples t-tests, Sobel mediation tests
method.outcome_variable: Perceived intimacy score (aggregated from multiple items)
method.independent_variables: Partner attention, material benefit, time spent, attention frequency level
method.control_variables: Scenario order; sex (examined but often dropped); study-specific factors

results.summary: Across all studies, participants reported significantly greater intimacy toward partners who paid them attention, even when no tangible benefits were provided. Attention effects were larger than benefit effects and mediated expectations of help, willingness to help, and interest in friendship.

metadata.original_paper_title: It's the attention that counts: interpersonal attention fosters intimacy and social exchange
